_readme: Onboarding pressure-test scenarios for Coach Dean.
Each case simulates a full onboarding conversation.
Run manually via the admin/trigger endpoint (dry_run: true) or use the conversation
exchanges below to walk through the SMS flow step by step.
Key things to watch for in every case:
- Does Dean extract the goal correctly (right bucket + right distance)?
- Does Dean ever repeat a question already answered?
- Does Dean say anything tonally weird (too formal, too robotic, generic)?
- Does Dean handle non-standard races without hallucinating the distance?
- Does the initial plan reflect the athlete's actual constraints?

cases.name: NYC Marathon First-Timer

persona.name: Sarah
persona.background: Never run a race. Runs occasionally, maybe 2 miles at a time.

conversation.user_says: Hi! I'm Sarah. I just signed up for the NYC Marathon in November and have no idea what I'm doing 😅
watch_for: Should NOT assume she has a time goal
Should welcome her warmly without being cheesy

conversation.user_says: I just want to finish honestly. I'd be thrilled to run the whole thing
watch_for: Should accept 'finish' as valid — no time goal is fine
Should not push for a specific time

conversation.user_says: I don't have Strava
watch_for: Should move on cleanly, not dwell on it

conversation.user_says: I run maybe 2-3 miles at a time a couple times a week
watch_for: Should recognize this is low base
Should NOT assign aggressive week 1 mileage

conversation.user_says: Monday, Wednesday, and Saturday work best for me
watch_for: Should confirm 3 days/week
Should not put runs on Tue/Thu/Fri/Sun

conversation.user_says: Nope I think that's everything!
watch_for: Clean close, should fire initial_plan

red_flags: Assigns more than 10-12 miles in week 1
Assigns tempo or interval work in week 1 for a beginner
Asks for a finish time goal after she said 'just finish'
Generic opener like 'Great! Let's get started'

cases.name: Boston Qualifier Chaser

persona.name: Marcus
persona.background: Has run 4 marathons, PR is 3:18. Needs sub-3:05 to BQ for his age group.

conversation.user_says: Hey Dean, I'm Marcus. I'm chasing a Boston qualifier at Chicago in October. Need to run sub 3:05.
watch_for: Should pick up the BQ context and time goal in one shot
Should NOT re-ask for time goal since he stated it

conversation.user_says: Yes I'm on Strava — linking now
watch_for: Smooth Strava link flow

conversation.user_says: 5 days a week, flexible on which days
watch_for: Should assign balanced 5-day split
Should include a long run on weekend

conversation.user_says: I have a slight history of plantar fasciitis in my right foot, nothing active right now
watch_for: Should capture injury note
Should not ignore it
Should not panic about it

red_flags: Re-asks for time goal after he stated sub-3:05 in his first message
Assigns easy-only training for someone at this level
Ignores the plantar fasciitis note in the initial plan
Uses generic VDOT paces without acknowledging his recent half time

cases.name: Western States 100

persona.name: Priya
persona.background: Trail ultrarunner. Has finished two 50Ks and one 50-miler. Training for Western States 100.

conversation.user_says: Training for Western States 100 in June. Got in through the lottery after 5 years of trying!
watch_for: Should recognize this is a 100-mile race, NOT a 100K
Should not apply beginner conservatism
The excitement/context is meaningful — Dean shouldn't ignore it

conversation.user_says: I've done two 50Ks and finished the Marin Ultra Challenge 50 miler last spring. Running about 50-55 miles a week right now with decent vert.
watch_for: Should NOT cap week 1 at 10 miles
Should recognize this athlete is ready for 100mi training, not base building
Should note back-to-back long runs are appropriate

conversation.user_says: I'd love to finish under 30 hours but honestly just want to make it to Auburn
watch_for: Should accept this gracefully
Finish goal is valid for 100s

conversation.user_says: Yep, I'm on Strava
6 days a week, I take Fridays off. Long runs Saturday and Sunday back to back.
watch_for: Should honor back-to-back long run preference
Should NOT put a run on Friday
6 days is appropriate for 100mi training

conversation.user_says: No I think we're good

red_flags: Caps week 1 at 10 miles for a 55mi/week athlete
No mention of vert / elevation in the plan
No back-to-back long runs in the structure
Treats this like a marathon plan
Assigns road-running style paces without acknowledging trail

cases.name: Bay to Breakers (Non-Standard Distance)

persona.name: Jordan
persona.background: Casual runner. Does 2-3 mile runs occasionally. Not competitive.
persona.goal_race: Bay to Breakers, San Francisco — May 2026
persona.notes: Bay to Breakers is ~7.5 miles (12K). Jordan doesn't know the exact distance.

conversation.user_says: I want to do Bay to Breakers in San Francisco in May! It's kind of a fun run but I've never done it
watch_for: Should NOT bucket this as a 5K or marathon
Should look up / correctly identify Bay to Breakers as ~7.5 miles
Should use goal_distance_miles = 7.46 (or similar) NOT null
This is a casual vibe race — Dean should match that energy

conversation.user_says: No time goal, just want to run the whole way without walking
watch_for: Should accept no-time-goal cleanly

conversation.user_says: Nah I don't use Strava
I run like 2-3 miles a couple times a week, nothing structured
watch_for: Plan should build to 7.5 miles, not 13.1

conversation.user_says: 3 days a week, any days are fine
No that's it!

red_flags: Calls this a 10K (it's 7.46mi, not 6.2mi) in the plan
Calls this a half marathon
Sets goal mileage as 13.1 instead of 7.5
Assigns tempo or interval work for a casual fun run
Dean doesn't know Bay to Breakers and asks what race it is (it should web-search)

cases.name: Air Force PT Test — 1.5 Mile Under 9:29

persona.name: Tyler
persona.background: Active duty Air Force. Decent base fitness from gym work, not a dedicated runner.
persona.goal_race: Air Force PT test — 1.5 mile run
persona.notes: This is a fitness test, not a race. Failing has real consequences.

conversation.user_says: I have my Air Force PT test in 6 weeks and need to run 1.5 miles in under 9:29. Currently running it around 11 minutes.
watch_for: Should extract goal_distance_miles: 1.5
Should understand the time constraint (6 weeks)
Should recognize the stakes without being dramatic
Goal pace: 9:29 / 1.5 = 6:19/mi — should use this

conversation.user_says: Under 9:29, that's the cutoff
watch_for: Should store 9:29 as the goal time for 1.5 miles

conversation.user_says: I don't have Strava
I run about 8-10 miles a week, mostly just easy stuff
watch_for: Should not overcomplicate this — 6-week plan needs to be focused

conversation.user_says: 5 days a week, mornings before PT
No that's it, just need to pass

red_flags: Treats this like a 5K plan (it's 1.5 miles — very different training)
Adds long runs or high volume — wrong for a 1.5-mile speed goal
Ignores the 6-week urgency
Uses marathon-style periodization
Goal pace math is wrong (should be ~6:19/mi)

cases.name: Absolute Beginner 5K

persona.name: Diane
persona.background: Hasn't run since high school. Signed up for a local 5K with her daughter in 10 weeks.

conversation.user_says: Hi, I'm Diane! I signed up for a 5K with my daughter in about 10 weeks. I haven't really run in... decades honestly 😬
watch_for: Should be warm and encouraging without being patronizing
Should not assume she's unfit — she just hasn't run

conversation.user_says: No time goal. I just want to run the whole thing without stopping — my daughter is fast and I don't want to embarrass her lol
watch_for: Tone should be fun/warm here

conversation.user_says: What's Strava?
watch_for: Should explain briefly and move on — should NOT make her feel behind for not knowing
Should not spend multiple messages on this

conversation.user_says: I don't really run. I walk a lot, maybe 30-40 minutes a day.
watch_for: Should recognize walk-jog intervals are the right starting point
Week 1 should NOT start with 3mi runs

conversation.user_says: 3 days a week. Tues, Thurs, and Saturday morning.
I also have mild knee pain sometimes, nothing serious but wanted to mention it
watch_for: Should acknowledge the knee and factor into plan
Should not alarm her
Should suggest strength work for knees

red_flags: Week 1 assigns continuous running for a true beginner
No walk-jog intervals
Knee pain mention is ignored in initial plan
Plan exceeds 5K distance in early weeks
Condescending or overly cautious tone

cases.name: Road Runner Moving to Trail 50K

persona.name: Ben
persona.background: Road marathon runner (PR 3:45). Wants to try trail running. Signed up for a trail 50K in 5 months.

conversation.user_says: Ben here. I've been running road marathons for a few years but I just signed up for the Marin Ultra Challenge 50K trail race in 5 months. First trail race ever.
watch_for: Should recognize this as a 50K (31 miles), not a marathon
Should note the trail transition in the plan
Should not treat him like a beginner — he has a solid base

conversation.user_says: I've run 4 marathons, PR is 3:45. I run about 35 miles a week right now. Never done a trail race.
watch_for: Should NOT cap week 1 at 10 miles for someone running 35/week
Should address trail-specific prep (vert, technical terrain, time-on-feet)
Power hiking mention is appropriate for a 50K newcomer

conversation.user_says: No time goal, just finish
Yeah I use Strava
5 days a week, back-to-back long runs on the weekend
watch_for: Back-to-back long runs are correct for ultra training — should honor it

conversation.user_says: No I think that covers it

red_flags: Treats this as a marathon plan
No mention of elevation/vert in the plan
Sandbangs week 1 to 20mi when he's running 35/week
No back-to-back long run structure
Assigns road paces for a trail race

cases.name: Injury Recovery — Stress Fracture

persona.name: Kevin
persona.background: Experienced marathoner (5 finishes). Had a metatarsal stress fracture. Just cleared to run last week after 4 months off.
persona.notes: 4 months total off. Very motivated but needs conservative approach.

conversation.user_says: Hi, I'm Kevin. I'm coming back from a stress fracture in my foot — just cleared by my doctor last week after 4 months off. I want to work toward a half marathon in about 3 months.
watch_for: Should route through injury_recovery OR half_marathon — either is acceptable
Should acknowledge the return-to-run context seriously
Should NOT assign aggressive volume in week 1

conversation.user_says: Metatarsal stress fracture in my right foot. 4 months off, no running at all. I was running about 40 miles a week before it happened. Doctor says I'm cleared but to take it easy.
watch_for: Should acknowledge the 4 months off means significant detraining
Should suggest a very gradual return — not jumping to 40mi/week
3 months to half marathon from 0 is doable but tight — should address this honestly

conversation.user_says: No time goal. Just getting back to running.
No Strava. Haven't touched it since I got hurt.
Zero right now. First week back.
watch_for: Week 1 should be extremely conservative — maybe 10-15 min easy runs
Should build very gradually given bone stress fracture history

conversation.user_says: 4 days a week works
Just hope to make it to the start line healthy

red_flags: Assigns more than 12-15 miles in week 1 for someone coming back from a bone injury
No mention of gradual return or bone loading caution
3-month half marathon plan is treated as normal — doesn't flag the timeline is tight
Ignores the stress fracture history in session design
No strength/mobility work included

cases.name: Vague Goal — 'Just Want to Get Faster'

persona.name: Aisha
persona.background: Runs 3-4 miles a few times a week. No race planned. Wants to get faster generally.

conversation.user_says: Hey I just want to get faster. No race in particular, just tired of being slow.
watch_for: Should handle the vague goal gracefully
Should ask a clarifying question — what does 'faster' mean to her?
Should NOT route her to marathon or 5K without her saying so
Should find out if there's a specific distance she runs

conversation.user_says: Like, I run 5Ks sometimes and just want to do them faster. Maybe break 30 minutes?
watch_for: Should update to 5K goal with sub-30 time target
Should not re-ask what she already answered

conversation.user_says: Yeah, sub-30 would be great
No Strava
Maybe 10-12 miles a week? I run like 3-4 miles 3 times a week
4 days a week, mornings before work
Nope!

red_flags: Buckets vague 'get faster' as marathon or some other specific race
Asks for race date when she said she has no race
Re-asks for time goal after she said sub-30 in the clarification
Doesn't adapt when she clarifies 5K mid-conversation

cases.name: Masters Runner — Boston Qualifier at 62

persona.name: Carol
persona.background: Lifelong runner. Marathon PR 3:42 from 12 years ago. Wants to qualify for Boston (needs sub-4:00 for her AG).

conversation.user_says: Hi Dean! I'm Carol, 62 years old. I've been running for 30 years and I want to qualify for Boston. My AG standard is 4:00 and my PR is 3:42 from 2012.
watch_for: Should recognize the age group context and the 4:00 standard
Should NOT ignore or minimize her experience
Should not be condescending about her age

conversation.user_says: Sub-4:00. Ideally closer to 3:55 to have a cushion for entry.
watch_for: Should use 3:55 as the working goal pace

conversation.user_says: Yes, on Strava
5 days, but I need one of those to be a rest or easy day — my body doesn't recover like it used to
watch_for: Should honor recovery needs for masters athlete
Should not assign 5 hard days

conversation.user_says: Just some mild hip tightness, nothing that stops me. I do yoga twice a week.
watch_for: Should capture yoga and hip tightness
Should include hip mobility work
Should not put runs on yoga days if she designates them

red_flags: Ignores her 30 years of experience and treats her like a beginner
Assigns 5 quality sessions per week for a masters runner
Doesn't mention recovery as a key training lever for her age
Uses her old PR paces (3:42) instead of her current fitness (4:00 goal)

cases.name: Spartan Beast (OCR — Non-Running Goal)

persona.name: Jake
persona.background: CrossFit athlete, 4 days/week. Does 3-mile runs occasionally. First Spartan Beast (~13 miles + obstacles).
persona.notes: CrossFit 4 days/week already, can only add 3 days of running

conversation.user_says: Hey I'm Jake. I do CrossFit and I'm signing up for a Spartan Beast in 4 months. It's like 13 miles with obstacles. I can handle the obstacles but my running sucks.
watch_for: Should bucket as half_marathon distance (~13mi) with race_name 'Spartan Beast'
Should understand this is an obstacle race, not a road half
Should note he already has a strength base from CrossFit

conversation.user_says: Just finish. Complete all the obstacles or do the penalty burpees.
No Strava
I run maybe 5-6 miles a week. My long run is like 3 miles. But I'm fit from CrossFit.
watch_for: Should acknowledge his CrossFit base — he's not a beginner athlete
Running volume should be built up to 13 miles over 4 months

conversation.user_says: 3 days of running, I do CrossFit Mon/Tue/Thu/Sat
watch_for: Should NOT put runs on Mon/Tue/Thu/Sat
Running days should be Wed/Fri/Sun or similar

conversation.user_says: No that's it

red_flags: Puts running sessions on CrossFit days (Mon/Tue/Thu/Sat)
Assigns strength work when he already does CrossFit
Treats him like a running beginner and ignores his athletic base
Makes the plan sound like a road half marathon plan

cases.name: Two Races — Stacked Goal

persona.name: Rachel
persona.background: Consistent runner, 25-30mi/week. Has a 10K in 8 weeks and a half marathon 7 weeks after that.
persona.goal_race: 10K in 8 weeks + half marathon 7 weeks later

conversation.user_says: I have two races — a 10K in 8 weeks and then a half marathon 7 weeks after that. Want to PR both.
watch_for: Should capture both races
Should understand the 10K comes first — build for 10K, then extend for half
Should NOT just plan for the half marathon and ignore the 10K

conversation.user_says: Sub-50 for the 10K and under 1:55 for the half if possible
Yep Strava
5 days, Mon through Fri with weekends usually free for long stuff
I also do yoga on Sunday mornings, so I'd rather not run then
watch_for: Should NOT put a run on Sunday
Yoga is a rest/recovery day

red_flags: Ignores the 10K and only builds toward the half
Puts a run on Sunday after she mentioned yoga
Doesn't mention the dual-peak structure in the plan rationale
10K in 8 weeks gets no race-specific sharpening

cases.name: Triathlete Running Leg Only

persona.name: Mei
persona.background: Competitive swimmer and cyclist. Running is the weakest discipline. Doing a relay — running the half marathon leg.
persona.notes: Swims 3x/week and bikes 2x/week already. Only wants run coaching.

conversation.user_says: Hi! I'm Mei. I'm doing a half ironman relay with friends — I'm running the half marathon leg in 5 months. I swim and bike already, just need help with the running.
watch_for: Should understand she only wants running coaching, not triathlon coaching
Should NOT recommend swim/bike sessions
Should note her aerobic base from swimming and cycling is strong

conversation.user_says: I'd love to finish under 2:15 — that's about 10:20/mi pace
No I don't track runs on Strava
I run maybe 6-8 miles a week but my cardio is really strong from swimming and biking
watch_for: Should not cap week 1 conservatively just because she runs little — her aerobic base is strong
Should factor in cross-training fitness

conversation.user_says: 4 days of running, I swim Mon/Wed/Fri and bike Tue/Thu
watch_for: Should not put runs on Swim days (Mon/Wed/Fri) or bike days (Tue/Thu) unless she's OK doubling
This is a real scheduling puzzle — Dean should ask or make a note

conversation.user_says: Nope, just focus on the running

red_flags: Recommends swim or bike sessions
Treats her as a running beginner despite strong aerobic base
Ignores the swim/bike schedule conflict when assigning run days
Plans for a full triathlon instead of relay running leg

cases.name: Primary Goal: Weight Loss (Half Marathon as Vehicle)

persona.name: David
persona.background: Non-runner. Joined a half marathon 'for motivation to lose weight.' The race is the vehicle, not the real goal.

conversation.user_says: I signed up for a half marathon in 6 months. Honestly the goal is really to lose weight and get in shape, the race is just something to work toward.
watch_for: Should acknowledge the weight loss motivation positively without making it weird
Should keep the focus on training — Dean is a running coach, not a nutritionist
Should not over-promise on weight loss outcomes

conversation.user_says: No time goal. Finish is fine.
Nope
I barely run. Maybe walk 20 minutes sometimes.
watch_for: Week 1 should be very conservative
Walk-jog intervals are appropriate

conversation.user_says: 3 days a week, weekdays whenever
I'm also trying to watch what I eat. Any advice on nutrition?
watch_for: Should acknowledge nutrition briefly and redirect to running — Dean is a running coach
Should NOT give detailed nutrition advice or meal plans
A brief 'I can help with running — for nutrition you'd want a dietitian' is perfect

red_flags: Goes deep on nutrition advice
Ignores the weight loss motivation entirely (it's motivationally important)
Assigns high mileage for a non-runner
Assigns no strength/mobility when this athlete clearly needs it

cases.name: Army APFT — 2 Mile Run, 4 Weeks

persona.name: Darius
persona.background: Army recruit. Needs to run 2 miles under 16:36 (passing standard for 17-21 age group). 4 weeks until test.
persona.notes: Failing has serious career consequences. He's currently at about 18:30.

conversation.user_says: I have my Army PT test in 4 weeks, 2-mile run, need to do it in under 16:36. I'm running it in about 18:30 right now.
watch_for: Should extract 2.0 miles and 16:36 goal time
Goal pace: 16:36 / 2 = 8:18/mi — should use this
4 weeks is short — plan should be focused and urgent
Need to cut 1:54 off a 2-mile — that's a significant improvement in 4 weeks

conversation.user_says: 16:36, that's the passing standard
No
I run about 15 miles a week with the unit. We do formation runs and stuff.
watch_for: Should factor in existing unit PT runs

conversation.user_says: 6 days a week, I'm on base
No just need to pass

red_flags: Assigns long runs or high volume — wrong for a 2-mile speed goal
Doesn't address the urgency of 4 weeks
Goal pace math is wrong
Treats this like a 5K plan

cases.name: International Athlete — Metric Units

persona.name: Camille
persona.background: French runner training in Paris. Uses km and min/km. Local 10K road race.

conversation.user_says: Bonjour! I'm Camille. I'm training for a 10K in Paris in 2 months. My goal is under 50 minutes.
watch_for: Should use metric units throughout since she's a metric runner
Should NOT convert everything to miles if she's thinking in km
Paces should be in min/km

conversation.user_says: Under 50 minutes, so 5:00/km pace
watch_for: Should store pace as min/km, not convert to min/mi

conversation.user_says: Yes Strava
4 days, Tuesday Wednesday Friday Sunday
Non, c'est tout!
watch_for: Should handle non-English gracefully — doesn't need to respond in French
Should understand 'that's all' in context

red_flags: Uses miles/min per mile throughout when she thinks in km
Doesn't recognize 5:00/km as her stated goal pace
Confused by the French sign-off

cases.name: High Volume Runner, New Baby, Time Crunch

persona.name: Tom
persona.background: Used to run 55-60mi/week. New baby 3 months ago. Now averaging 20mi/week, max 4 days. Wants to race a half marathon PR.

conversation.user_says: Hey. Had a baby 3 months ago and my running has taken a hit. I'm doing about 20 miles a week now, used to do 55+. I want to train for a half marathon PR — have one in 12 weeks.
watch_for: Should empathize briefly without dwelling on it
Should meet him where he IS (20mi/week) not where he was (55mi/week)
Should not push volume he can't sustain with a newborn

conversation.user_says: My PR is 1:38, want to get under 1:35
Yeah, on Strava
Max 4 days a week. No back-to-back long days — baby doesn't allow it on weekends honestly.
watch_for: Should honor the 4-day limit
Should NOT put a long run Saturday AND Sunday
Should acknowledge the real-world constraint respectfully

conversation.user_says: Just need something realistic. I can't train like I used to.
watch_for: Dean should confirm this is achievable — it is, with quality over quantity

red_flags: Assigns 50+ miles per week (his old level)
Back-to-back long runs when he said he can't do them
Ignores current 20mi/week base and sandbangs too much
Doesn't acknowledge the life constraint at all

cases.name: General Fitness — No Race, No Goal

persona.name: Fatima
persona.background: Wants to run more and build a habit. No race in mind. Just wants to feel better.

conversation.user_says: I just want to start running. No race or anything. I just want to be someone who runs.
watch_for: Should handle 'no race' gracefully — this is valid
Should NOT push her toward signing up for a race if she doesn't want one
Should ask what 'running more' looks like to her — frequency? distance? how she feels?

conversation.user_says: No Strava
I basically don't run right now. I walk places but that's it.
watch_for: Week 1 should be very accessible — maybe run/walk intervals
Should not overwhelm her

conversation.user_says: I want to try 3 days a week to start
I guess I'd love to eventually run a 5K without stopping but that feels far away
watch_for: Should seize this as a soft goal and use it to frame the plan
Should not immediately pivot to race registration — just use it as motivation

red_flags: Pushes her to sign up for a race when she explicitly said no race
Assigns high mileage for a non-runner
Doesn't give her a short-term milestone to work toward
Makes running sound hard and complicated

cases.name: Hardrock 100 — Extreme Vert Ultra

persona.name: Alex
persona.background: Serious ultrarunner. 70mi/week with significant vert. Has done multiple 100Ks. Training for Hardrock 100 (33,000ft gain).

conversation.user_says: Training for Hardrock 100 this July. It's a 100-mile mountain race with 33,000 feet of climbing in Colorado. I've done two 100Ks and run about 70 miles a week.
watch_for: Should recognize 33,000ft vert is extremely specific and important
Should NOT apply any beginner conservatism
Should acknowledge this is an elite-tier goal — Hardrock has a lottery and a DNF-rate
Vert-specific training (hiking, power hiking, specific ascent/descent) is critical

conversation.user_says: Two 100K finishes — Golden Gate Dirty 30 and Santa Barbara 9 Trails. Running 65-70 miles a week with 6,000-8,000 feet of vert. I hike the steep stuff.
watch_for: Should not cap week 1 at 10 miles — this person runs 70/week
Back-to-back long runs are essential
Power hiking should be explicitly part of the plan
Altitude acclimatization note (Hardrock is 11,000+ ft) is worth mentioning

conversation.user_says: Under 40 hours if I'm being honest with myself, but finishing is the real goal — 48hr cutoff
Yes, Strava
7 days a week, doubles on some days. I take one easy day — usually Tuesday.
watch_for: Should honor the 7-day structure
Tuesday easy day should be respected
Doubles are appropriate at this level

conversation.user_says: Just need help structuring the big picture — I know what I'm doing day to day
watch_for: Should trust his expertise, not over-coach basics

red_flags: Week 1 capped at 10 miles for someone running 70/week
No mention of vert or elevation work
No back-to-back long run structure
Ignores power hiking as a training tool
Treats this like a road marathon plan
Doesn't mention altitude as a factor for Hardrock

cases.name: Beer Mile — Novelty / Edge Case

persona.name: Chris
persona.background: Casual runner. Wants to do a Beer Mile (run 1 mile, drinking a beer each quarter mile) with friends.
persona.notes: This is testing how Dean handles a clearly non-standard, novelty race with good humor.

conversation.user_says: I want to do a Beer Mile in 3 weeks. It's a mile run where you drink a beer every quarter mile. Goal is to go under 8 minutes.
watch_for: Should handle this with good humor — it's a real event
Should bucket as 'mile' goal
Should NOT be preachy about alcohol
Goal pace: 8:00/mi — should use this for running training
Dean can't really train the beer-drinking part, just the running

conversation.user_says: Under 8 minutes total
No
I run maybe 10 miles a week, nothing structured
3 days a week, I'm flexible
Nope just gotta run fast and not throw up
watch_for: Dean should ideally match the humor here — one liner is fine
Should not lecture about health
3-week plan for a 1-mile race should be appropriately short and focused

red_flags: Lectures about alcohol / refuses to help
Misses the humor and treats it with complete seriousness
Assigns a 12-week training program for a 3-week goal
Ignores that this is 1 mile, not a 5K
